text: Hello, Catherine here from BBC Learning English.
zh: 你好,我是BBC英语教学的凯瑟琳。

text: Just so you know, this programme is from the BBC Learning English archive
zh: 提醒大家一下,本节目来自BBC英语教学的档案库,

text: and was first broadcast in September 2016 on our website. And now, on with the show!
zh: 最初于2016年9月在我们的网站上首播。现在,让我们开始节目吧!

text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Alice.
zh: 大家好,欢迎收听《六分钟英语》。我是艾丽斯。

text: And I'm Neil. Could you lend me some dosh, Neil. Sure. How much do you need?
zh: 我是尼尔。尼尔,能借我点“钱”吗?当然,你需要多少?

text: A couple of smackers. You're sounding a bit strange today, Alice.
zh: 就几张“票子”。艾丽斯,你今天说话有点怪怪的。

text: Yes, I know, Neil. Slang, or informal language used by a particular group, is the subject of today's show.
zh: 是的,尼尔。俚语,也就是特定群体使用的非正式语言,是今天节目的主题。

text: And I was just demonstrating a couple of slang words that mean money.
zh: 我刚才只是在示范几个表示“钱”的俚语词。

text: Dosh is a general term for money and a smacker is a British pound or US dollar.
zh: dosh是“钱”的通用说法,而smacker指的是一英镑或一美元。

text: OK, so Cockney rhyming slang is a type of slang.
zh: 好的,伦敦腔押韵俚语就是俚语的一种。

text: It's a coded language invented in in the 19th century by Cockneys, so they could speak in front of the police without being understood.
zh: 这是19世纪伦敦土话使用者发明的一种暗语,好让他们能在警察面前交流而不被听懂。

text: And still on the subject of money, I have a question for you, Alice. Oh, OK.
zh: 接着说到钱的话题,我有个问题问你,艾丽斯。哦,好的。

text: What's Cockney rhyming slang for money? Is it a bread, b honey or c dough?
zh: 伦敦腔押韵俚语里“钱”怎么说?是a. bread,b. honey,还是c. dough?

text: I think it's a bread. I bet you didn't know, Neil, that I'm a Cockney.
zh: 我觉得是bread。我打赌你不知道吧,尼尔,我其实是个伦敦土著。

text: Oh, don't Adam and Eve it, Alice. That's a pork pie!
zh: 哦,别“信不信”了,艾丽斯。那简直是“撒谎”!

text: Adam and Eve means believe and pork pie means lie. Actually, you're right, I'm not a cockney.
zh: Adam and Eve的意思是“相信”,pork pie的意思是“谎言”。其实你说得对,我不是伦敦土著。

text: To be considered a cockney, you need to be born within hearing distance of the bells of St Mary le Beau church in what is now the City of London.
zh: 要被认定为伦敦土著,你得出生在能听到St Mary le Bow教堂钟声的范围内,那地方就是现在的伦敦城。

text: Indeed. Now, slang, as we've said, is colloquial or informal language
zh: 没错。我们说过,俚语是口语化、非正式的语言,

text: and it's characteristic of specific social groups.
zh: 是特定社会群体的特征。

text: We We usually use it in informal conversation rather than in writing or more formal situations like a job interview.
zh: 我们通常在非正式对话中使用它,而不是在书面语或求职面试这类更正式的场合。

text: We change the way we speak so that what we say is appropriate for a particular situation.
zh: 我们会根据场合调整说话方式,使表达更得体。

text: So you surprised me earlier, Alice, by talking about dosh and smackers because it didn't seem appropriate for presenting the show.
zh: 所以艾丽斯,你刚才说dosh和smacker的时候让我挺意外,因为这似乎不太适合主持节目。

text: Slang use is often frowned upon or disapproved of.
zh: 使用俚语常常会被人皱眉或不认可。

text: Let's listen to Jonathan Green, a lexicographer of slang, talking about who uses slang and how this has changed.
zh: 我们来听听俚语词典编纂者乔纳森·格林,谈谈谁在使用俚语,以及这种情况是如何变化的。

text: Here he is on the Radio 4 programme Word of Mouth. Jonathan Green, Radio 4 Programme
zh: 以下是他在Radio 4节目《口耳相传》中的发言。乔纳森·格林,Radio 4节目

text: Slang does have a bad reputation, and I would say this comes from its earliest collection, which was of criminal slang
zh: 俚语确实名声不太好,我认为这源于它最早的记录,也就是16世纪,

text: in the 1500s, in the 16th century, and it was associated with bad people. And inevitably that has lingered.
zh: 也就是1500年代收集的犯罪俚语,当时它跟坏人联系在一起。这种印象不可避免地延续了下来,

text: but now, in the last 40 -50 years, it's changed.
zh: 但在过去的四五十年里,情况已经变了。

text: The definitions tend to stress different and jocular, funny, humorous, inventive, that kind of thing.
zh: 现在的定义更倾向于强调“不同”、“诙谐”、“有趣”、“幽默”、“富有创意”这类特点。

text: So, we have records of 16th century slang in collections
zh: 所以,我们有16世纪俚语的记录,收录在

text: or dictionaries, words used by criminals as a code so they could talk without being understood.
zh: 词典或汇编中,那是罪犯用来编暗语交流、不被外人听懂的词汇。

text: And this bad reputation has lingered or been slow to disappear.
zh: 这种不好的名声延续了下来,消散得很慢。

text: But for the last 50 years, we've been using slang
zh: 但在过去的50年里,我们使用俚语

text: to be funny and creative, as well as to show belonging to a particular group.
zh: 是为了显得幽默、有创意,同时也表明自己属于某个特定群体。

text: And apparently we're very creative when talking about drinking and being drunk.
zh: 而且显然,在谈论喝酒和醉酒这件事上,我们特别有创意。

text: The slang word booze, meaning alcohol, comes from the 13th century Dutch word boozen.
zh: 俚语词booze,意思是酒,来源于13世纪荷兰语boozen。

text: And there are hundreds of slang expressions to talk about drink and being drunk.
zh: 关于喝酒和醉酒的俚语表达有成百上千种。

text: On the sauce, in your cups, half cut, hammered, squiffy, tipsy, wasted, legless
zh: 比如on the sauce、in your cups、half cut、hammered、squiffy、tipsy、wasted、legless,

text: and many, many more that are far too rude to mention in this programme.
zh: 还有很多很多因为太粗俗而不便在本节目中提及的说法。

text: Yes. So, while these terms might not be strictly acceptable
zh: 是的。所以,虽然这些说法在正式场合中

text: or appropriate in formal contexts, they aren't offensive, they are often amusing and help people bond in social groups.
zh: 未必被完全接受或合适,但它们并不冒犯人,反而常常很有趣,能帮助人们在社交群体中建立联系。

text: By contrast, swear words or profanity means rude language that offends or upsets people.
zh: 相比之下,脏话或粗话指的是冒犯或惹恼别人的粗俗语言。

text: And I'm not going to give any examples because that would be inappropriate and impolite, Alice. this. OK.
zh: 我就不举例了,艾丽斯,因为那样做不太合适,也不礼貌。好的。

text: Let's listen now to Jonathan Green and presenter Michael Rosen talking about jargon – another type of in -group language.
zh: 我们现在来听乔纳森·格林和主持人迈克尔·罗森谈谈行话——另一种群体内部语言。

text: Jargon, what I would call is small -o occupational, small -p professional slang.
zh: 行话,我称之为“小写的o职业性、小写的p专业性”俚语。

text: It's closed -off environment. You get legal jargon, you get naval jargon.
zh: 它是一个封闭的环境。你会有法律行话,也会有海军行话。

text: I've been reading Patrick O 'Brien recently and that's a wash with futter plates and fiddying the deck.
zh: 我最近在读帕特里克·奥布莱恩的书,里面到处都是futtock plate(肋骨板)和fiddying the deck(整理甲板)之类的词。

text: This is Radio 4, Jonathan. Be careful. Jonathan Green in another segment of the BBC Radio 4 programme, Word of Mouth.
zh: 这里可是Radio 4,乔纳森,小心点。乔纳森·格林在BBC Radio 4节目《口耳相传》的另一段发言中说道。

text: So he says jargon is occupational and professional, meaning people speak it at work, for example lawyers and sailors.
zh: 所以他说行话是职业性和专业性的,意思是人们在工作中使用它,比如律师和水手。

text: A futtock plate is, I believe, an iron plate attached to the top of a ship's mast, but I don't know much about this
zh: futtock plate,我相信,是安装在船桅顶部的一块铁板,不过我对这个话题了解不多,

text: subject. That's the idea though. Jargon is the technical language belonging to a specific group.
zh: 大概就是这个意思。行话就是属于特定群体的专业术语。

text: And to outsiders, this jargon is often hard to understand.
zh: 对局外人来说,这种行话往往很难理解。

text: Yes, and here in the studio I can use all the radio jargon that I like.
zh: 是的,在这个演播室里,我可以尽情使用各种广播行话。

text: Look at my faders here, Alice, going down and up and up and up. Just testing
zh: 艾丽斯,看看我这儿的推子,一会儿往下,一会儿往上,又往上。只是在测试

text: our levels. Come on, leave the fader alone.
zh: 我们的音量。喂,别碰推子。

text: It controls the level of sound on a studio deck. Now it's time for the answer to today's quiz question, Neil.
zh: 它控制着演播室调音台上的声音音量。现在是揭晓今天问题答案的时间了,尼尔。

text: I asked you, what's Cockney rhyming slang for money? Is it a bread, b honey or c dough?
zh: 我问你,伦敦腔押韵俚语里“钱”怎么说?是a. bread,b. honey,还是c. dough?

text: And I said a bread. And you were right, Alice.
zh: 我说是bread。你答对了,艾丽斯。

text: Cockney rhyming slang uses just the first word of a phrase that rhymes with the word we're trying to disguise.
zh: 伦敦腔押韵俚语只使用与要隐藏的词押韵的短语中的第一个词。

text: So money becomes bread and honey, but we just say bread.
zh: 所以money变成了bread and honey,但我们只说bread。

text: OK, so let's recap on the words we've learnt today.
zh: 好的,那我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇。

text: They are slang, dosh,
zh: 它们是:slang(俚语)、dosh(钱),

text: smacker, cockney rhyming slang, colloquial,
zh: smacker(钱)、cockney rhyming slang(伦敦腔押韵俚语)、colloquial(口语化的),

text: frowned upon, lingered, booze, swear, profanity, jargon.
zh: frowned upon(不被认可的)、lingered(延续)、booze(酒)、swear(咒骂)、profanity(粗话)、jargon(行话)。

text: Well, that's the end of today's 6 Minute English. Please join us again soon. Bye bye. Goodbye.
zh: 好了,今天的《六分钟英语》就到这里。欢迎大家continue收听。再见。再见。
